hypotheses: H1. A negative correlation will be found between secure attachment style and a high level of trait anxiety.
H2. A positive correlation will be found between secure attachment style and openness to experiences.
H3. A negative relation will be found between levels of trait anxiety and leadership experiences.
H4. A positive relation will be found between openness to experiences and leadership experiences.
claim.hypotheses_location: Pages 752-753: hypotheses H1 and H2 stated after Fig. 1 under 'The two preliminary hypotheses'; H3 and H4 stated in section 'Inﬂuence of the basic components on the development of leaders via experience' (pages 752-753).
claim.statement: Secure attachment style formed in early childhood explains the formation of basic components (low trait anxiety and openness to experiences) which in turn affect leader development via experiences in leadership roles.
claim.statement_location: Abstract (end of abstract) and introduction/research question section (paragraph starting 'The research question, then, is this: Does a secure attachment style predict...') around page 752.
claim.study_type: Observational (cross-sectional survey using self-report questionnaires; correlational study analyzed with SEM)

data.source: Self-report questionnaires administered to soldiers in the Israel Defense Forces (IDF). Instruments included an attachment style questionnaire (anxiety and avoidance dimensions combined to derive security), Trait Anxiety Inventory (STAI), an abbreviated Openness to Experiences questionnaire based on NEO-PI-R, and a Leadership-Shaping Experiences (LSE) questionnaire.
data.wave_or_subset: Participants were drawn from four companies (two armored corps companies, two infantry companies). A pilot study was conducted earlier with 195 recruits; main sample from end of basic training.
data.sample_size: 286 soldiers volunteered (initial sample). Analyses reported with varying Ns per measure (e.g., Table 1: secure attachment n=245, trait anxiety n=229, openness n=239, leadership experiences n=240). SEM diagrams report N≈238 (models show N=238 and N=236 for some models).
data.notes: Language difficulties led to exclusion of some new immigrants (10–20% in various platoons). Instruments were abbreviated/adapted for the army setting (e.g., 24-item Openness measure). Parceling was used to create three composite indicators per latent construct for SEM. Reliability (Cronbach's alpha) and pairwise Ns differ across measures.

method.description: Cross-sectional survey of soldiers using self-report questionnaires measuring attachment tendencies (anxiety and avoidance dimensions), trait anxiety (STAI), openness to experience (abbreviated OE), and retrospective leadership-shaping experiences (LSE). Reliability checks and correlational analyses were conducted. Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) using AMOS 5 was applied: a measurement model (with item parceling into three indicators per latent construct) was tested first, followed by testing of a structural causal model and five alternative models. Fit indices (χ2, CFI, NFI, NNFI, GFI, RMSEA) were used to evaluate model fit.
steps: Conduct pilot study to test instruments (administer questionnaires, follow-up interviews, retest for stability).
Administer self-report questionnaires to main sample of soldiers toward end of basic training (volunteers from four companies).
Compute scale scores and internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha) for each instrument; inspect correlations between constructs.
Parcel items for each multi-item construct into three random composites to serve as indicators for latent variables.
Specify and test measurement model in SEM to confirm latent constructs (reporting factor loadings and fit indices).
Specify hypothesized structural model (secure attachment → trait anxiety and openness → leadership experiences) and estimate via maximum likelihood in AMOS 5.
Test five alternative structural models (including direct paths and alternative causal directions) and compare model fit (χ2, fit indices, ECVI).
Report standardized path coefficients (betas), significance levels, and variance explained (R^2) for leadership experiences; interpret best-fitting model.
method.models: Structural Equation Modeling (confirmatory SEM) using AMOS 5. Measurement model with parceling; maximum likelihood estimation. Fit assessed with χ2 goodness-of-fit, CFI, NFI, NNFI, GFI, RMSEA, ECVI. Six structural models tested (primary hypothesized model plus five alternatives).
method.outcome_variable: Leadership experiences (Leadership-Shaping Experiences, LSE) — measured by a 7-item questionnaire on a 5-point Likert scale assessing responsibility and influence in home, school, and social contexts; aggregate score range and mean reported.
method.independent_variables: Secure attachment style (derived from anxiety and avoidance dimensions; lower anxiety and avoidance => secure), Trait anxiety (STAI total score), Openness to experiences (24-item OE questionnaire derived from NEO-PI-R).
method.tools_software: AMOS 5 for SEM. Instruments referenced: STAI (Spielberger et al., 1970), NEO-PI-R (Costa & McCrae) and Hebrew adaptations; parceling procedure described; no other software explicitly stated.

results.summary: The hypothesized model (secure attachment → lower trait anxiety and higher openness → greater leadership experiences) fit the data well and was supported. Secure attachment was strongly negatively related to trait anxiety and positively related to openness. Trait anxiety negatively predicted leadership experiences, and openness positively predicted leadership experiences. The primary model explained about 19% of variance in leadership experiences. Alternative models were tested; some fit less well or yielded non-significant direct paths (e.g., direct path from secure attachment to leadership experiences was non-significant when added).

numerical_results.p_value: χ2(50)=57.3, p = .22; all fit indexes > .90; RMSEA reported significant (p < .05)

metadata.original_paper_title: Attachment and leader's development via experiences
